I have enough grounding. Now let me make the brief/PDR decision and author scenarios.

**Decision on brief/PDR:**

- **lead-i18 (implicit CWD resolution)** is genuinely new architectural surface — a new addressing mode that sits alongside (not replaces) name-based addressing from PDR-007. This warrants **PDR-009** because (a) it makes an architectural choice (walk-up from CWD reading `.claude/shop/{name,type}.md` rather than other discovery patterns), (b) it has a non-obvious "scope" question (which subcommands), and (c) PDR-007 explicitly committed to "shops are addressed by canonical name" and an implicit-CWD lookup is best framed as extending that with a derived-name form, not contradicting it. Future readers will absolutely re-ask "why does CWD resolution work? wasn't PDR-007 about name-based addressing?" so a PDR is the right vehicle.

- **lead-33r (canonical settings.json hooks)** is a tightening within **brief 003**'s scope item A — that brief committed the canonical settings.json shape and its bootstrap-managed treatment. New scenarios pin a content-level invariant ("no placeholder substrings in hook commands; canonical content is bare `shop-msg prime`"). Brief 003 is the right anchor — no new brief.

**Implicit-CWD scope decision:** I'll bundle to all subcommands taking `--bc/--lead` (the existing mutually-exclusive arg pair: `prime`, `pending`, `read`, `respond`, `watch`, `send`). Rationale: the failure mode is identical across all of them (placeholder embedded in hook → wrong shop named → wrong shop addressed); fixing only `prime` papers over the canonical-content fix while leaving the same trap for any agent that types `shop-msg pending inbox` in a BC repo. The mechanism (walk-up reading `.claude/shop/{name,type}.md`) is shared infrastructure; pinning it once at one subcommand and not the others creates an asymmetry that's harder to remember than the uniform rule. **For `send`** specifically, the implicit lookup resolves the *sender* (the `--from` identity, currently implicit at the BC respond level); the *recipient* must still be named explicitly. PO commits: implicit lookup resolves shop identity for `--bc`/`--lead` when those flags are omitted; addressing flags meaning "the OTHER shop in this exchange" (e.g., `shop-msg send --bc <recipient>`) are out of scope for implicit resolution. I'll pin this in scenarios.

Let me also check whether lead.json should add `shop-msg prime` alongside `bd prime`. Per the BC primer I just read: "Run `bd prime`... Run at session start... Arm the Monitor on `shop-msg watch --bc <name>`" — the BC explicitly runs both. Per the lead-primer in this repo: "this shop uses bd (beads)..." and "shop-msg watch --lead <name>" arming. Both leads and BCs benefit from `shop-msg prime` (DSN reachability check). PO judgment: **the canonical settings.json carries BOTH hooks for both shop types** as two SessionStart entries — `bd prime` (operational hygiene) AND `shop-msg prime` (messaging-reachability check). They're independent and short-lived; both belong.

Now author scenarios. The feature dirs are `messaging` (for lead-i18) and `templates` (for lead-33r). Per the naming convention I see, scenarios are numbered sequentially within a directory.

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# PDR-009 — Implicit CWD-based shop resolution in `shop-msg`

**Status:** decided (2026-05-27)
**Authors:** dstengle, Claude (lead-po)
**Anchored to:** [PDR-007](007-path-to-name-addressing-migration.md)
(name-based addressing as the canonical addressing model); user-driver
observation 2026-05-27: a session opened inside `repos/shopsystem-docs/`
errored with `shop name '{{SHOP_NAME}}' is not registered`, because the
canonical BC `.claude/settings.json` template ships
`shop-msg prime --bc {{SHOP_NAME}}` and the bootstrap surface pours
`.claude/*` byte-for-byte (per PDR-003 alt F, scenarios
`2b9bd9c82017b0c6` / `cad9ccb5b462978d`).

## The question

`shop-msg` today requires explicit shop addressing on every invocation
via the mutually-exclusive `--bc <name>` / `--lead <name>` flag pair. Per
PDR-007 this addressing is canonically *by name*, with paths gone. The
canonical BC SessionStart hook needs the shop's own name to pass to
`shop-msg prime`. The shop's name is already the single source of truth
on disk at `.claude/shop/name.md` (with `.claude/shop/type.md` next to
it). How should the hook get the name into the invocation?

Two top-level shapes:

1. **Pour-time substitution.** Bootstrap reads `name.md` (or takes a
   shop-name argument) and writes the literal name into
   `settings.json` at pour time — `shop-msg prime --bc shopsystem-docs`.
2. **Implicit CWD-based resolution.** Bootstrap pours
   `settings.json` byte-for-byte from the canonical (no substitution);
   the canonical hook command is bare `shop-msg prime`; `shop-msg`
   itself, when invoked without addressing flags, discovers the shop
   identity by walking up from CWD looking for `.claude/shop/name.md`
   and `.claude/shop/type.md`, the same way `bd` discovers `.beads/`.

## Options considered

### Option A — Pour-time substitution into settings.json

`shop-templates bootstrap` substitutes `{{SHOP_NAME}}` in the canonical
`settings.json` body at pour time, writing the literal shop name into
the target's `.claude/settings.json`. The canonical template carries the
placeholder; the bootstrap pour rule for `.claude/*` becomes
*byte-for-byte except for `.claude/settings.json`, which substitutes*.

**Pros:**

- No new resolution machinery in `shop-msg`. The CLI continues to take
  shop addressing explicitly on every invocation, and the hook simply
  passes a literal name.
- Substitution mechanism could be reused for any future placeholder
  needs in `settings.json`.

**Cons:**

- **Duplicates the shop name into a second file.** The name already
  lives in `.claude/shop/name.md` (per the four-typed-files
  CLAUDE.md import-graph pinned by scenario 83 in `features/templates/`).
  After pour, the name lives in *both* `name.md` and the literal
  `command` string of `settings.json`. If a shop is ever renamed, two
  files have to change in lockstep; staleness in one of them silently
  routes to the wrong shop.
- **Contradicts the explicit pour discipline.** PDR-003 alt F established
  that `.claude/*` files pour byte-for-byte; the precedent decision
  ruled against pour-time substitution. Re-introducing substitution
  for `settings.json` carves out an exception that future contributors
  will read as license to add more exceptions. The byte-for-byte rule's
  value is its uniformity.
- **Does nothing for ad-hoc invocations.** An agent that types
  `shop-msg pending inbox` (no addressing flags) inside a BC repo
  still has to type the BC name. The convenience of "knowing what shop
  you're in" exists nowhere in the CLI.
- **Latent bug surface persists.** The canonical hook still embeds
  shop identity; any future caller pouring `settings.json` without
  going through the substitution path (a copy-paste, a manual edit, a
  different bootstrap entry point) re-introduces the exact bug this
  PDR exists to fix.

### Option B — Implicit CWD-based resolution in `shop-msg`

`shop-msg` gains a CWD-based shop discovery mechanism: when invoked
without `--bc` or `--lead`, it walks up from the current working
directory looking for `.claude/shop/name.md` and `.claude/shop/type.md`,
reads the canonical name and shop type from those files, and resolves
the shop identity via the existing registry as it would for an explicit
`--bc <name>` / `--lead <name>` invocation. Explicit flags continue to
work unchanged; they always win over the implicit lookup.

The canonical `.claude/settings.json` hook command becomes bare
`shop-msg prime`. Bootstrap pours `settings.json` byte-for-byte, no
substitution.

**Pros:**

- **Eliminates duplication of shop identity.** `name.md` and `type.md`
  remain the sole on-disk source of truth for shop identity. The hook
  carries no identity at all — it only carries the intent ("prime this
  shop, whatever shop this is").
- **Preserves PDR-003 alt F byte-for-byte pour.** No exception to the
  pour rule; the bug fix lives entirely in `shop-msg`, not in the
  pour pipeline.
- **Mirrors a familiar pattern.** `bd` discovers `.beads/` from CWD via
  walk-up. Operators and agents already model "tool discovers project
  identity from CWD" because beads behaves that way. Implicit CWD
  resolution in `shop-msg` reads as an extension of an established
  pattern, not as a new concept.
- **Ad-hoc invocations stop being painful.** An agent that types
  `shop-msg pending inbox` inside a BC repo gets the right answer for
  this BC. The "I know what shop I'm in" affordance applies to every
  subcommand, not just the SessionStart hook.
- **Closes the latent bug class.** No canonical content embeds shop
  identity, so no future copy-paste of canonical content can revive the
  `{{SHOP_NAME}}`-shaped class of bug.
- **PDR-007 compatible.** Name-based addressing is preserved: `shop-msg`
  still resolves shops by canonical name via the registry. The implicit
  lookup *derives* the canonical name from `.claude/shop/name.md` and
  then enters the same registry-resolution path the explicit flag uses.
  Path-based addressing remains gone.

**Cons:**

- **New machinery in `shop-msg`.** The CLI grows a CWD-walk-up step
  before flag parsing's resolution path runs. This is a small surface
  (mirrors the well-understood `bd` pattern), but it is non-zero.
- **Two resolution paths.** Explicit-flag and implicit-CWD both exist.
  The mutual-exclusion / precedence rule (explicit always wins; implicit
  fires only when neither flag is passed) must be pinned in scenarios
  and tested. Manageable, but a real test-surface cost.
- **Implicit failure modes need clear errors.** Running `shop-msg prime`
  in a directory that is not inside any shop (no walk-up parent contains
  `.claude/shop/`) must produce a clear, non-zero-exit error that tells
  the operator which addressing strategy to use. Silent failure (or
  routing to a wrong shop) would be worse than today.

### Option C — Both: pour-time substitution AND implicit-CWD

Both mechanisms ship: bootstrap substitutes the canonical hook with the
literal shop name (so the hook works regardless of CWD context), AND
implicit-CWD ships as a separate affordance for ad-hoc invocations.

**Pros:**

- Defense in depth: the hook works even if the implicit-CWD resolution
  ever broke; implicit-CWD still helps for ad-hoc commands.

**Cons:**

- Carries both Option A's "duplicate identity into two files" cost and
  Option B's "new machinery" cost.
- Two ways for the canonical hook to be correct creates ambiguity about
  which is *the* convention. Future canonical content has to decide
  which path to use; precedent gets muddier with every addition.
- Defense in depth here is solving for a failure mode that does not
  meaningfully exist — implicit-CWD's failure mode is "directory has no
  `.claude/shop/`," and that condition is the *bootstrap not having
  run*, which means substitution would also have nothing to substitute.

## Decision

**Option B — Implicit CWD-based resolution in `shop-msg`.**

Rationale:

1. **Single source of truth for shop identity.** The shop name lives
   at `.claude/shop/name.md`; the shop type lives at `.claude/shop/type.md`.
   No other file on disk needs to carry shop identity. The bug that
   surfaced this PDR is exactly the failure mode of duplicating identity
   into a second file and letting one go stale (`{{SHOP_NAME}}` literal
   in the poured hook). Option B closes the bug at the architectural
   level, not at the pour-pipeline level.

2. **PDR-003 alt F's byte-for-byte rule is preserved.** That rule's
   value is uniformity — every `.claude/*` file is poured the same way.
   Carving out an exception for `settings.json` (Option A) creates a
   precedent for further exceptions, which is precisely what alt F
   ruled against. The fix lives in `shop-msg`, not in a new pour
   special-case.

3. **Mirrors `bd`'s established pattern.** Operators and agents already
   model "tool discovers project identity by walking up from CWD."
   `bd prime` works that way; `bd ready`, `bd show`, etc. all work
   that way. Implicit CWD in `shop-msg` extends a pattern that exists
   uniformly across the project's adjacent tools rather than introducing
   a novel mechanism. The cognitive load is near-zero for anyone who
   already uses `bd`.

4. **PDR-007 compatible — extends, does not reverse.** Implicit CWD
   resolution does not re-introduce path-based addressing. The CWD walk
   produces a canonical *name* (read from `.claude/shop/name.md`), and
   that name flows into the existing registry-resolution path that
   PDR-007 made canonical. Path-based addressing remains removed;
   the implicit-CWD lookup is a *name-derivation* step in front of
   the name-based addressing model PDR-007 committed to.

5. **Closes the bug class, not just the bug.** Option A fixes the
   immediate `{{SHOP_NAME}}` substitution bug, but any future canonical
   content that needs shop identity revives the same trap. Option B
   removes the trap: no canonical content has reason to embed shop
   identity, so no future canonical content can reintroduce the bug.

## Scope of the implicit lookup

The implicit lookup applies to **every `shop-msg` subcommand that
currently accepts the `--bc <name>` / `--lead <name>` mutually-exclusive
addressing flag pair** to identify the *invoking* shop. The PO commits
to bundling rather than scope-tight-to-`prime`:

- `prime`, `pending`, `read`, `respond`, `watch` — implicit lookup
  resolves the invoking shop's identity when no addressing flag is
  passed.
- `send` — `send` carries two identities: the sender (the shop running
  the command) and the recipient (the addressed `--bc <name>` /
  `--lead <name>` flag). The implicit lookup applies **only to the
  sender**. The recipient is always explicit. A `send` invocation
  inside a lead shop with no flags addresses *no one*; the recipient
  flag is required.

The "sender is implicit, recipient is explicit" rule is the natural
extension of PDR-007's invariant (every directional addressing decision
goes through a canonical name) to the implicit case (the canonical name
of the sender is *derived* from CWD; the canonical name of the recipient
is always *named*).

Rationale for bundling rather than scoping tight to `prime`:

- **Uniformity over selective fix.** Tight-to-`prime` papers over the
  canonical-content fix while leaving the same trap for any agent that
  types `shop-msg pending inbox` (or any other subcommand) in a BC
  repo. A uniform rule — "any `shop-msg` invocation without addressing
  flags resolves the invoking shop from CWD" — is easier to remember
  and reason about than "implicit only for `prime`; explicit
  everywhere else."
- **The mechanism is shared.** The walk-up that reads
  `.claude/shop/{name,type}.md` is one routine, used identically by
  every subcommand. Pinning it at one subcommand and not the others
  creates an asymmetry without a corresponding architectural reason.
- **The cost is the same.** The test surface for implicit-CWD is the
  walk-up routine plus the precedence rule. Both exist regardless of
  how many subcommands honor the implicit lookup.

## Error behavior

When implicit lookup fires (no addressing flags) and the walk-up reaches
the filesystem root without finding `.claude/shop/name.md` + `type.md`,
`shop-msg` exits non-zero with a clear diagnostic naming both addressing
strategies: the operator can either `cd` into a shop directory or pass
an explicit `--bc <name>` / `--lead <name>` flag.

When implicit lookup fires but the walk-up finds `name.md` but not
`type.md` (or vice versa), `shop-msg` exits non-zero — the two files
together name the shop's identity; one without the other is corruption,
not a partial state to tolerate.

When implicit lookup fires and the resolved name does not appear in
the registry, the existing "shop not registered" error fires (same
behavior as explicit `--bc <unregistered-name>`).

## What this leaves open

1. **The walk-up's exact termination condition.** Walk up until
   `.claude/shop/` is found, or until the filesystem root is reached.
   Whether the walk also terminates at a git boundary (e.g., a parent
   `.git/` directory not matching the shop) is the Architect's call.
   PO leaning: terminate at filesystem root only; let the registry
   provide the final correctness check. But this is realization detail.
2. **Whether `shop-msg`'s --help should mention the implicit lookup.**
   PO commits the behavior; help-text is the Architect's call.
3. **Whether implicit lookup should also apply to a future
   `--from <name>` flag on `send` (sender-naming)** if such a flag is
   ever introduced. Out of scope for this PDR; current `send` has no
   `--from` flag.

## Cross-references

- [PDR-007](007-path-to-name-addressing-migration.md) — established
  name-based addressing as canonical; this PDR extends it with a
  name-*derivation* step that runs in front of the existing
  name-resolution path.
- [PDR-003](003-claude-md-update-propagation.md) — established
  byte-for-byte pour of `.claude/*` files (alt F); this PDR preserves
  that rule by moving the fix into `shop-msg` instead of into pour.
- [brief 006](../briefs/006-messaging-name-registry-and-lead-inbox.md)
  — the registry surface implicit-CWD's resolved name flows into.
- [brief 003](../briefs/003-event-driven-shop-activation.md) —
  the SessionStart hook whose canonical content this PDR enables to
  carry no embedded shop identity. The companion canonical-settings
  scenarios live alongside this PDR's `shop-msg` scenarios in
  brief 003's scope.
- Adjacent pattern: `bd`'s discovery of `.beads/` from CWD via
  walk-up — the established mirror for this PDR's mechanism.

```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-27 14:44:16Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```gherkin
Feature: Canonical ".claude/settings.json" hook commands contain no "{{...}}" placeholder substrings

  Per PDR-003 alt F (scenarios 2b9bd9c82017b0c6 / cad9ccb5b462978d), bootstrap pours ".claude/*" files byte-for-byte from canonical package data with no substitution. A canonical hook command containing a "{{...}}" placeholder substring therefore survives the pour as a literal "{{...}}" sequence in the consumer's ".claude/settings.json", and the Claude Code harness then executes the literal sequence — silently producing a wrong-shop or unrecognized-name error. The canonical template surface must therefore commit, at the package-data level, that no hook command body contains placeholder-shaped content.

  Per PDR-009 (decided 2026-05-27), shop identity is never embedded in canonical content; "shop-msg" resolves the invoking shop's identity from CWD by walking up looking for ".claude/shop/name.md" and ".claude/shop/type.md". This eliminates any architectural reason for a canonical hook command to embed shop identity via placeholder substitution.

  Scenario Outline: every inner-hook "command" string in the canonical ".claude/settings.json" template for "<shop_type>" contains no occurrence of the substring "{{" and no occurrence of the substring "}}"
    When I ask the "shop-templates" package for the canonical ".claude/settings.json" template for shop type "<shop_type>" through its public template-access surface
    And the returned body is parsed as JSON
    Then for every top-level hook-event key under "hooks" (including but not limited to "SessionStart" and "PreCompact"), and for every element of that hook-event's array, and for every entry of that element's inner "hooks" array, the entry's "command" string contains no occurrence of the substring "{{" and no occurrence of the substring "}}"

    Examples:
      | shop_type |
      | bc        |
      | lead      |

```

    Given an existing git repository at a target directory "<target>" that was previously bootstrapped as a "<shop_type>" shop named "<shop_name>"
    And the file at "<target>/.claude/settings.json" contains an inner-hook entry under "hooks.SessionStart" whose "command" string is the stale literal "shop-msg prime --bc {{SHOP_NAME}}"
    When I invoke the "shop-templates" update entry point against target "<target>"
    Then the exit code is 0
    And after the invocation no inner-hook entry under "hooks.SessionStart" in "<target>/.claude/settings.json" has a "command" string containing the substring "{{SHOP_NAME}}"
    And after the invocation exactly one inner-hook entry under "hooks.SessionStart" in "<target>/.claude/settings.json" has a "command" string equal to the literal value "shop-msg prime"
    And after the invocation "<target>/.claude/settings.json" equals the current canonical ".claude/settings.json" template for shop type "<shop_type>" byte-for-byte

    Examples:
      | shop_type | shop_name              | target                       |
      | bc        | shopsystem-docs        | /tmp/example-bc-shop         |
      | lead      | shopsystem-product     | /tmp/example-lead-shop       |

```

## Report

**Brief/PDR decision.** Authored **PDR-009** at `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/pdr/009-implicit-cwd-shop-resolution.md` because the architectural choice (CWD walk-up reading `.claude/shop/{name,type}.md`) is non-obvious enough that "why does CWD resolution work given PDR-007 made everything name-based?" will absolutely be re-asked. PDR-009 extends PDR-007 (it derives a canonical name from CWD and feeds it into the same registry path) rather than reversing it. No new brief needed for **lead-33r** — those scenarios pin a content-level invariant within brief 003 scope item A's canonical-settings.json surface.

**Scope judgment (PDR-009).** Bundled implicit lookup to all subcommands taking `--bc`/`--lead`. Send carries a recipient that remains explicit; sender resolves implicitly. Rationale in the PDR's "Scope of the implicit lookup" section.

**Hook composition judgment (lead-33r).** Canonical settings.json for BOTH shop types ships `bd prime` AND `shop-msg prime` as two distinct SessionStart entries. BC primer already documents the BC-side composition; PDR-009 brings lead canonical into the same shape.

**Sufficiency check.** Every scenario carries explicit Given/When/Then (or canonical-interrogation When/And/Then like scenarios 75/96), pins one observable behavior with measurable outcomes (exit code, parsed-JSON predicate, byte-for-byte equality, diagnostic content), and avoids constraints-without-outcomes.

**Name in the command:** `shopsystem-docs` — that's the canonical name from `repos/shopsystem-docs/.claude/shop/name.md`. So your hand-edit is:

```json
"command": "shop-msg prime --bc shopsystem-docs"
```

**Registration:** already done. `shop-msg registry list | grep shopsystem-docs` returns:
```
shopsystem-docs /workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-docs bc
```

This was set up during the bootstrap (lead-n78) via `shop-msg registry add shopsystem-docs /workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-docs`. That's the canonical surface — manual step, not auto-registered. The registry lives in postgres (shared across all shop-msg invocations in this devcontainer), so the docs BC will see its own entry from inside its repo dir too.

So once you swap the literal `{{SHOP_NAME}}` for `shopsystem-docs` in `repos/shopsystem-docs/.claude/settings.json`, `shop-msg prime --bc shopsystem-docs` will resolve and the SessionStart hook will report the pending `lead-km9` assignment cleanly. Just remember it's a workaround — the proper fix lands when lead-33r's re-pour ships and replaces this with the bare `shop-msg prime` form.

## Three BC clarifies are pending in the lead inbox. All three are scope questions. Respond to each via `shop-msg respond clarify` (or whatever the canonical clarify-response surface is — `shop-msg --help` if unsure). Operate from CWD `/workspaces/shopsystem-product`. Beware CWD drift — pin paths.

### 1. lead-km9 (shopsystem-docs)

Read the full clarify with `shop-msg read outbox --bc shopsystem-docs --work-id lead-km9`. Summary:

- The five adoption-doc scenarios assigned under `lead-km9` include `b0fe0666bcaa2398` (end-to-end adoption arrival — adopter follows the walkthrough, lead container running, BCs registered, dispatch succeeds).
- The BC says this scenario's Given ("an adopter has executed every step the walkthrough names, in order, on a host with Docker installed") can't be established inside its pytest-bdd harness (no real adopter host, no real Docker lifecycle, no real Claude Code session).
- They ask: (a) is `b0fe0666` meant to be pinned by this BC's BDD suite, or does it live elsewhere (separate end-to-end harness or manual checklist)? (b) If here, translate to file-content checks (which would overlap `8c55526c` agent-executable structure and `7ac28115` entry doc) or stub with `@manual` step defs?
- Their stated principle: "I do not want to fabricate a step def that vacuously passes."

**PO judgment to make:** Decide whether `b0fe0666` (i) stays here translated into structural file-content checks, (ii) gets an `@manual`-tagged step-def stub with explicit out-of-band-verification semantics, (iii) moves out of `lead-km9`'s scope and gets re-anchored in a separate higher-level harness/checklist, or (iv) gets retired/superseded outright by `8c55526c` + `7ac28115`. Provide your reasoning in the clarify response and, if you change the assigned scenario set (retire / supersede / re-anchor), reflect it in the appropriate feature file under `features/docs/` and reference the change in your response.

### 2. lead-1t2 (shopsystem-messaging)

Read with `shop-msg read outbox --bc shopsystem-messaging --work-id lead-1t2`. Summary:

- The `request_bugfix` description pins that a BC cannot emit both `work_done` and `mechanism_observation` for the same `work_id`. The fix introduces `allow_multi_type=True` in `insert_message`.
- BC notes that NO scenario pins the SUCCESS path: a BC emitting `work_done` followed by `mechanism_observation` for the same work_id, with BOTH responses visible in the lead inbox.
- BC proposes a specific tightening scenario (full Gherkin in the clarify body) and asks: should it be added to `respond_routes_to_lead_inbox.feature` or a new feature file?

**PO judgment to make:** Accept the proposed tightening scenario (it pins exactly the regression risk the BC names), and choose the file. Either decide `respond_routes_to_lead_inbox.feature` (proximity to existing routing scenarios) or a new file (e.g., `multi_type_responses.feature`). Add the scenario to the lead's canonical features under `features/messaging-registry/` (or wherever the lead-side canonical scenarios for messaging live — check `features/` layout). Cross-reference it in the clarify response so the BC knows where to find it after dispatch.

### 3. shopsystem-templates-918 (sender is shopsystem-bc-launcher, despite the work_id prefix)

Read with `shop-msg read outbox --bc shopsystem-bc-launcher --work-id shopsystem-templates-918`. Summary:

- Existing scenarios pin the happy path for copying `.claude.json` from host `~/.claude/.claude.json` into the launched BC container (scenario `85202a4076f45fba`).
- No scenario pins the missing-file case. Today the launcher issues `cp` unconditionally and ignores the return code — so if the host file is absent, the container starts and the operator has no indication credentials weren't propagated.
- BC proposes three candidate behaviors: (a) fast-fail before `docker run` (mirror missing-dir scenario `4ba55450ec351623`), (b) warn in stderr/stdout but continue, (c) silent skip with documented behavior. They ask which the lead intends.

**PO judgment to make:** Pick one of the three behaviors (or a defensible variant) and author a tightening scenario in the appropriate feature file under `features/` matching the chosen semantics. Justification in your clarify response should note why (consistency with `4ba55450ec351623` is a strong argument for (a); operator-friendliness is a strong argument for (b); silent is rarely right for a credentials path). Don't punt — pick.

## After authoring / responding

- For each clarify, also append a notes line to the corresponding lead-shop bead (`bd update <id> --notes "..."`) summarizing the resolution and pointing to any new feature files.
- Commit your work with a `feat(po):` style message. Do not push (router handles session close). Do not dispatch new `shop-msg send` messages — that's the architect's surface.

Out of scope for you: BC code, architect-side dispatch, work_done reconciliation, ADRs/architecture decisions.

    respond             write a BC response message

                        unless the DB is unreachable. Use --bc <name> for

    registry            manage the shop name registry (name-based addressing)

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Run bd prime for workflow context

```text
- `bd formula list` - See available workflow templates
- `bd mol pour <name>` - Start structured workflow from formula

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

## Persistent Memories (3)

Stored via `bd remember`. Update in place with `bd remember --key <key> "new content"`. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`. Remove with `bd forget <key>`.

### bd-cwd-drift-trap
bd uses CWD to locate .beads/, NOT a fixed repo path. When CWD drifts into a sibling repo (e.g. repos/shopsystem-templates/), bd operations hit THAT repo's bd, not the lead shop's bd — and you see different prefixes (shopsystem-templates-*) and different issue sets. Symptom this session (2026-05-22): I cd'd into repos/shopsystem-templates early on, never cd'd back, then spent multiple turns convinced sub-agents were 'confabulating' lead-* IDs that bd 'didn't have'. They weren't confabulating — they were in the right CWD (lead repo) and I was in the wrong one. Workaround: pin every bd/shop-msg/git invocation to /workspaces/shopsystem-product as CWD (use cd or git -C), and treat 'bd show X says no issue found' as a CWD-check trigger before assuming the issue doesn't exist.

### pyyaml-1-1-coerces-github-actions-on-key
PyYAML 1.1 coerces GitHub Actions 'on' key to boolean True. In step defs parsing workflow YAML with yaml.safe_load(), use wf.get(True) not wf.get('on'). The shopsystem-devcontainer BC uses a _get_workflow_on() helper (tests/conftest.py:820) as the fix. Consider ruamel.yaml (YAML 1.2) to avoid this permanently.

### shopsystem-bc-launcher-assert-docker-run-includes-flag
shopsystem-bc-launcher: assert_docker_run_includes_flag step does not assert container_name against the run_cmd — latent fragility if multiple containers run in same test. BC noted this in lead-0eu work_done summary. Queue a request_bugfix once lead-90z and lead-cyo are processed.
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
✓ lead-6c5 [BUG] · [bug] request_bugfix: messaging test suite clobbers production registry on every run   [● P1 · CLOSED]
Owner: David Stenglein · Assignee: David Stenglein · Type: bug
Created: 2026-05-21 · Started: 2026-05-21 · Updated: 2026-05-22
Close reason: Closed

DESCRIPTION
The shopsystem-messaging pytest suite overwrites production shop_registry entries on every run. In tests/conftest.py the session-scoped _ensure_session_lead fixture (lines 87-114) calls registry_add('shopsystem-product', str(root.resolve()), shop_type='lead') and registry_add('shopsystem product', str(root.resolve()), shop_type='lead') with a tmp_path as the shop_root. registry_add uses ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE (storage.py lines 630-640), so these calls overwrite existing production entries pointing to /workspaces/shopsystem-product. After any test run, shop-msg registry list shows 'shopsystem product' and 'shopsystem-product' pointing at /tmp/pytest-of-vscode/pytest-NNN/... paths. The teardown at line 128 only removes the random test-lead-session-* entry; the two named entries are never cleaned up. Similarly, the step-def at line 3439 (given_bc_registered) registers any bc_name passed in from a feature file under a tmp_path with no teardown. Operational effect: shop-msg pending outbox --lead 'shopsystem product' silently returns empty; resolve_lead_shop() routes BC responses to a tmp path. Recovery requires manually re-running shop-msg registry add after every test run. Fix: the test suite must not upsert well-known production names. Either (a) use a separate database or schema for tests (SHOPMSG_DSN already supports this via env var), (b) save and restore the original registry values around the session fixture, or (c) add a finalizer that removes the two named entries and re-inserts the original values. Source: tests/conftest.py lines 87-128 and 3439-3448; storage.py registry_add lines 622-642.
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
✓ lead-m0a [BUG] · Lead subagent templates missing Claude Code YAML frontmatter   [● P1 · CLOSED]
Owner: David Stenglein · Type: bug
Created: 2026-05-22 · Updated: 2026-05-22
Close reason: Closed

DESCRIPTION
The canonical templates for lead-po.md and lead-architect.md (in repos/shopsystem-templates/src/shop_templates/templates/) lack the YAML frontmatter (---\nname: ...\ndescription: ...\n[tools: ...]\n---) that Claude Code requires to register a markdown file as an invokable subagent. As a result, when shop-templates bootstrap pours a lead shop, the resulting .claude/agents/lead-po.md and .claude/agents/lead-architect.md are present on disk but unregistered: the runtime reports 'Agent type "lead-po" not found' (and likewise for lead-architect) when the router attempts to dispatch.

Empirical demonstration: from /workspaces/shopsystem-product, an Agent invocation with subagent_type=lead-po failed with the registration error above; the available-agents list contained only the runtime defaults (claude-code-guide, Explore, general-purpose, Plan, statusline-setup) and none of the lead subagents.

This blocks the entire lead-shop role-isolation pattern documented in lead-primer.md §'Who you are — router for lead-po and lead-architect subagents.' The router cannot delegate PO or architect work, which means briefs/PDRs/scenarios cannot be PO-authored and request_bugfix/assign_scenarios/request_maintenance dispatches cannot be architect-composed.

The BC subagent templates in the same directory (bc-implementer.md, bc-reviewer.md) DO carry the required frontmatter — they are the pattern to match. The fix is to add an analogous frontmatter block to lead-po.md and lead-architect.md whose name/description fields accurately describe each lead-shop role's invocation criteria.

This bug was likely introduced in commit 88ca83c ('agents: lead-po and lead-architect subagents (PDR-002 path a)').

NOTE on dispatch protocol: this request_bugfix is being filed router-direct rather than architect-composed because the architect subagent is itself one of the unreachable subagents — composing the dispatch through the proper vehicle requires the very capability this bugfix restores. Router-direct is the one-off needed to bootstrap the architect role itself; future dispatches return to architect-composed once shopsystem-templates ships the fix and we re-pour.
```
</details></tool-use>

809	def insert_bc_response(
810	    lead_root: str,
811	    bc_root: str,
812	    work_id: str,
813	    message_type: str,
814	    payload: dict[str, Any],
815	) -> None:
816	    """Insert a BC response into the lead shop's inbox namespace.
817	
818	    This replaces the old ``insert_message(..., direction='outbox', ...)``
819	    call that the respond commands previously used.  Under the new routing
820	    (Brief-006 scope C / PDR-007 / lead-e9x) BC responses are delivered
821	    TO the lead's inbox rather than written to the BC's own outbox.
822	
823	    Two writes happen atomically:
824	    1. ``(bc=lead_root, work_id, direction='inbox', message_type)`` — the
825	       response in the lead's namespace.  This is the primary delivery target
826	       and the row that ``shop-msg read inbox --lead`` and
827	       ``shop-msg pending inbox --lead`` inspect.
828	    2. ``(bc=bc_root, work_id, direction='outbox', message_type)`` — a local
829	       marker in the BC's own namespace so that ``shop-msg pending inbox --bc``
830	       can determine that the BC has responded to this work_id (the existing
831	       ``NOT EXISTS outbox`` query in ``query_pending_inbox`` uses this).
832	
833	    Collision semantics: if the lead-inbox row already exists, ``CollisionError``
834	    is raised (no second write attempted).  The BC-side marker is written
835	    idempotently (ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) since it is a secondary artefact.
836	
837	    After a successful insert, fires NOTIFY on the lead-inbox channel so
838	    ``shop-msg watch --lead`` can wake up.
839	    """
840	    lead_bc_id = _bc_id(lead_root)
841	    bc_bc_id = _bc_id(bc_root)
842	    payload_json = json.dumps(payload)
843	
844	    with _connect() as conn:
845	        with conn.cursor() as cur:
846	            # Check collision on the lead-inbox row first (primary target).
847	            cur.execute(
848	                """
849	                SELECT 1 FROM messages
850	                WHERE bc = %s AND work_id = %s
851	                  AND direction = 'inbox' AND message_type = %s
852	                LIMIT 1
853	                """,
854	                (lead_bc_id, work_id, message_type),
855	            )
856	            if cur.fetchone() is not None:
857	                raise CollisionError(
858	                    f"response already exists: lead={lead_root!r} work_id={work_id!r} "
859	                    f"message_type={message_type!r}"
860	                )
861	
862	            # 1. Write to lead inbox.
863	            cur.execute(
864	                """
865	                INSERT INTO messages (bc, work_id, direction, message_type, payload)
866	                VALUES (%s, %s, 'inbox', %s, %s::jsonb)
867	                ON CONFLICT (bc, work_id, direction, message_type) DO NOTHING
868	                """,
869	                (lead_bc_id, work_id, message_type, payload_json),
870	            )
871	            lead_rows = cur.rowcount
872	
873	            # 2. Write BC-side marker (for pending inbox --bc tracking).
874	            cur.execute(
875	                """
876	                INSERT INTO messages (bc, work_id, direction, message_type, payload)
877	                VALUES (%s, %s, 'outbox', %s, %s::jsonb)
878	                ON CONFLICT (bc, work_id, direction, message_type) DO NOTHING
879	                """,
880	                (bc_bc_id, work_id, message_type, payload_json),
881	            )
882	        conn.commit()
883	
884	    if lead_rows == 0:
885	        raise CollisionError(
886	            f"response already exists: lead={lead_root!r} work_id={work_id!r} "
887	            f"message_type={message_type!r}"
888	        )
889	
890	    # Fire NOTIFY on the lead's inbox channel so ``watch --lead`` wakes up.
891	    from psycopg import sql
892	    channel = _lead_inbox_slug(lead_root)
893	    with psycopg.connect(_get_dsn(), autocommit=True) as nconn:
894	        nconn.execute(
895	            sql.SQL("NOTIFY {channel}, {payload}").format(
896	                channel=sql.Identifier(channel),
897	                payload=sql.Literal(work_id),
898	            )

  lines 87-114 calls registry_add(''shopsystem-product'', <tmp_path>, shop_type=''lead'')
  and registry_add(''shopsystem product'', <tmp_path>, shop_type=''lead''). registry_add
  uses ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE (storage.py lines 622-642), so these upserts silently
  overwrite the production entries pointing to /workspaces/shopsystem-product. Teardown
  at conftest.py line 128 only removes the random test-lead-session-* entry; the two
  named production entries are never restored. Similarly, the given_bc_registered
  step-def at line 3439 registers any bc_name from a feature file under a tmp_path
  with no finalizer. After a pytest run: shop-msg registry list shows ''shopsystem
  product'' -> /tmp/pytest-of-vscode/pytest-NNN/...; resolve_lead_shop() routes BC
  responses to a tmp path; shop-msg pending outbox --lead ''shopsystem product'' silently
  returns empty. Recovery currently requires manually re-running shop-msg registry
  add. Fix: tests must not upsert well-known production names. Options: (a) isolate
  tests to a separate DB/schema via SHOPMSG_DSN (already supported), (b) save original
  registry values before overwriting and restore in finalizer, or (c) use only synthetic
  random names (never ''shopsystem product'' or ''shopsystem-product'') and add a
  finalizer to remove them. Source: tests/conftest.py lines 87-128, 3439-3448; storage.py
  lines 622-642.'
scenarios: []

✓ lead-3g0 [BUG] · [bug] request_bugfix: shop-msg pending inbox --bc returns empty despite unconsumed rows   [● P1 · CLOSED]
Owner: David Stenglein · Assignee: David Stenglein · Type: bug
Created: 2026-05-21 · Started: 2026-05-21 · Updated: 2026-05-22
Close reason: Closed

DESCRIPTION
shop-msg pending inbox --bc <name> resolves the BC path via resolve_shop_name() (storage.py:732-747) and then runs query_pending_inbox(bc_root) (storage.py:277-302). That query filters by bc = <resolved_path>. When the registry entry for the BC name has been overwritten by test fixtures (Bug D), resolve_shop_name() returns a tmp path and the query finds zero matching rows even though the real inbox row exists under the production path. Additionally, query_pending_inbox uses NOT EXISTS (outbox row) as the pending predicate, but the messages table also has a consumed column; if consumed semantics are used on the inbox side in future this predicate diverges. Observed: shop-msg pending inbox --bc shopsystem-bc-launcher returned empty while DB row (id=7, bc=/workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-bc-launcher, work_id=lead-1wv, direction=inbox, consumed=False) existed. Note: lead-yaj covers only the outbox direction (pending outbox --lead filtering); this is the separate inbox direction bug. Source: src/shop_msg/storage.py lines 277-302 and src/shop_msg/cli.py lines 168-182 (_resolve_bc) and 585-596 (_cmd_pending_inbox).
```
</details></tool-use>

payload:
  work_id: lead-6c5

  description: 'The shopsystem-messaging pytest suite overwrites production shop_registry
    entries on every run, breaking all operational shop-msg commands until manual
    recovery. Root cause: the session-scoped _ensure_session_lead fixture in tests/conftest.py
    lines 87-114 calls registry_add(''shopsystem-product'', <tmp_path>, shop_type=''lead'')
    and registry_add(''shopsystem product'', <tmp_path>, shop_type=''lead''). registry_add
    uses ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE (storage.py lines 622-642), so these upserts silently
    overwrite the production entries pointing to /workspaces/shopsystem-product. Teardown
    at conftest.py line 128 only removes the random test-lead-session-* entry; the
    two named production entries are never restored. Similarly, the given_bc_registered
    step-def at line 3439 registers any bc_name from a feature file under a tmp_path
    with no finalizer. After a pytest run: shop-msg registry list shows ''shopsystem
    product'' -> /tmp/pytest-of-vscode/pytest-NNN/...; resolve_lead_shop() routes
    BC responses to a tmp path; shop-msg pending outbox --lead ''shopsystem product''
    silently returns empty. Recovery currently requires manually re-running shop-msg
    registry add. Fix: tests must not upsert well-known production names. Options:
    (a) isolate tests to a separate DB/schema via SHOPMSG_DSN (already supported),
    (b) save original registry values before overwriting and restore in finalizer,
    or (c) use only synthetic random names (never ''shopsystem product'' or ''shopsystem-product'')
    and add a finalizer to remove them. Source: tests/conftest.py lines 87-128, 3439-3448;
    storage.py lines 622-642.'

## Blocker report — three clarify responses cannot land on the wire

### The blocker

`shop-msg respond clarify --bc <name> --work-id <work_id> --question <text>` is BC-side direction (BC writes its clarify question into its own outbox, addressed to the lead). There is no lead-side `shop-msg send clarify_response` (or equivalent) in the current CLI. This is the same protocol gap previously documented as `lead-d6o` and again named in the `lead-gua` bd note: *"no native lead→BC clarify-response vehicle in shop-msg send."*

Even when run with BC arguments, the storage layer rejects the write:

```
$ shop-msg respond clarify --bc shopsystem-docs --work-id lead-km9 --question "..."
shop-msg respond clarify: refusing to overwrite existing response for work_id='lead-km9'

$ shop-msg respond clarify --bc shopsystem-messaging --work-id lead-1t2 --question "test"
shop-msg respond clarify: refusing to overwrite existing response for work_id='lead-1t2'

$ shop-msg respond clarify --bc shopsystem-bc-launcher --work-id shopsystem-templates-918 --question "test"
shop-msg respond clarify: refusing to overwrite existing response for work_id='shopsystem-templates-918'
```

Per `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-messaging/src/shop_msg/cli.py:230-277`, `_cmd_respond_clarify` calls `insert_bc_response` and catches `CollisionError`. Collisions happen because each work_id already has a lead→BC inbox row (the assign or bugfix that triggered the clarify), and the storage check at `storage.py:197` is also at issue per `lead-1t2`.

The role-template instruction "respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc <name> --work-id <work_id> ...`" therefore does not match the deployed CLI. Per the task's explicit guidance ("If you discover...`shop-msg respond clarify` has a constraint you can't meet... stop and report the blocker rather than working around it"), I am stopping.

### What IS ratified and ready (uncommitted in working tree)

**Clarify 1 — `lead-km9` / shopsystem-docs**

- `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/docs/02-walkthrough-end-state-is-lead-plus-bcs-ready-to-dispatch.gherkin` — prior PO's translation ratified. The translation correctly addresses condition 2 of the BC's sufficiency check (steps must be establishable in-harness) by moving from runtime-outcome assertions to walkthrough-content assertions. The four Then steps each pin a separately verifiable named-section property, and conditions 1, 3, 4 hold.
- New hash (naive `scenarios hash` of body alone): `8a3cf335034105e8`. NOTE: this is NOT the canonical wrapped-form hash. question: "The scenarios pin the happy path for copying .claude.json (scenario 85202a4076f45fba:\

  \ of: the command exits non-zero with stderr containing a description, OR the command\
  \ exits zero and stdout includes a warning that .claude.json was not found>. Which\
  \ behavior does the lead intend?"

```text
400	def _build_scenario_payload(
401	    path_str: str, feature_title: str, bc_tag: str
402	) -> ScenarioPayload:
403	    """Read a scenario body file, wrap it with the standard Feature header
404	    and tags, and hash the wrapped result. Shared between assign_scenarios
405	    and request_bugfix because both messages embed the same
406	    ScenarioPayload shape.
407	
408	    lead-018 tightened the ScenarioPayload schema so that
409	    `hash == canonical_hash(gherkin)`. The `gherkin` field stores the
410	    wrapped text (Feature header + tag line + body), so we must hash the
411	    wrapped text — hashing the inner body alone would put the CLI on the
412	    wrong side of the schema invariant.
413	
414	    Because the canonicalization rule strips `@scenario_hash:` lines
415	    before hashing, the chicken-and-egg of "the tag line includes the
416	    hash we're about to compute" resolves naturally: we can construct
417	    the wrapped text using the eventual hash as a placeholder, hash it,
418	    then replace the placeholder — but the simpler form below works
419	    because the canonicalization strips `@scenario_hash:<anything>`
420	    lines unconditionally. So we build the wrapped text with a sentinel
421	    hash, compute the canonical hash of that string (the sentinel line
422	    is dropped by canonicalization), and emit the same wrapped string
423	    with the now-known hash substituted in. The substitution does not
424	    perturb the canonical hash because both forms differ only in the
425	    stripped `@scenario_hash:` line.
426	    """
427	    body = Path(path_str).read_text()
428	    sentinel = "0" * 16
429	    sentinel_tags = [f"@scenario_hash:{sentinel}", f"@bc:{bc_tag}"]
430	    sentinel_tagged = (
431	        f"Feature: {feature_title}\n"
432	        f"\n"
433	        f"  {' '.join(sentinel_tags)}\n"
434	        f"  {body}\n"
435	    )
436	    scen_hash = _compute_scenario_hash(sentinel_tagged)
437	    tags = [f"@scenario_hash:{scen_hash}", f"@bc:{bc_tag}"]
438	    tagged = (
439	        f"Feature: {feature_title}\n"
440	        f"\n"
441	        f"  {' '.join(tags)}\n"
442	        f"  {body}\n"
443	    )
444	    return ScenarioPayload(hash=scen_hash, tags=tags, gherkin=tagged)
445	
446	
447	def _cmd_send_request_bugfix(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int:
448	    bc_root = _resolve_bc(args)
449	
450	    scenario_files = list(args.scenario_file or [])
451	    # --feature-title and --bc-tag are conditionally required: only when
452	    # at least one --scenario-file is supplied. argparse cannot express
453	    # "required iff" directly, so enforce post-parse here.
454	    if scenario_files and (args.feature_title is None or args.bc_tag is None):
455	        print(
456	            "shop-msg send request_bugfix: --feature-title and --bc-tag are "
457	            "required when --scenario-file is supplied",
458	            file=sys.stderr,
459	        )
```

---

## Blocker report — three clarify responses cannot land on the wire

### The blocker

`shop-msg respond clarify --bc <name> --work-id <work_id> --question <text>` is BC-side direction (BC writes its clarify question into its own outbox, addressed to the lead). There is no lead-side `shop-msg send clarify_response` (or equivalent) in the current CLI. This is the same protocol gap previously documented as `lead-d6o` and again named in the `lead-gua` bd note: *"no native lead→BC clarify-response vehicle in shop-msg send."*

Even when run with BC arguments, the storage layer rejects the write:

```
$ shop-msg respond clarify --bc shopsystem-docs --work-id lead-km9 --question "..."
shop-msg respond clarify: refusing to overwrite existing response for work_id='lead-km9'

$ shop-msg respond clarify --bc shopsystem-messaging --work-id lead-1t2 --question "test"
shop-msg respond clarify: refusing to overwrite existing response for work_id='lead-1t2'

$ shop-msg respond clarify --bc shopsystem-bc-launcher --work-id shopsystem-templates-918 --question "test"
shop-msg respond clarify: refusing to overwrite existing response for work_id='shopsystem-templates-918'
```

Per `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/repos/shopsystem-messaging/src/shop_msg/cli.py:230-277`, `_cmd_respond_clarify` calls `insert_bc_response` and catches `CollisionError`. Collisions happen because each work_id already has a lead→BC inbox row (the assign or bugfix that triggered the clarify), and the storage check at `storage.py:197` is also at issue per `lead-1t2`.

The role-template instruction "respond via `shop-msg respond clarify --bc <name> --work-id <work_id> ...`" therefore does not match the deployed CLI. Per the task's explicit guidance ("If you discover...`shop-msg respond clarify` has a constraint you can't meet... stop and report the blocker rather than working around it"), I am stopping.

### What IS ratified and ready (uncommitted in working tree)

**Clarify 1 — `lead-km9` / shopsystem-docs**

- `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/docs/02-walkthrough-end-state-is-lead-plus-bcs-ready-to-dispatch.gherkin` — prior PO's translation ratified. The translation correctly addresses condition 2 of the BC's sufficiency check (steps must be establishable in-harness) by moving from runtime-outcome assertions to walkthrough-content assertions. The four Then steps each pin a separately verifiable named-section property, and conditions 1, 3, 4 hold.
- New hash (naive `scenarios hash` of body alone): `8a3cf335034105e8`. NOTE: this is NOT the canonical wrapped-form hash. The CLI computes the hash over the wrapped form (`Feature: <title>\n  @scenario_hash:<sentinel> @bc:<tag>\n  <body>\n`) per `cli.py:_build_scenario_payload` (lines 400-444). The canonical hash will be computed by the Architect at dispatch time using the same `Feature: End-user adoption documentation (brief 007 v1)` wrapper used in the original `lead-km9` send. Old hash `b0fe0666bcaa2398` is superseded.
- Resolution narrative for the wire (drafted, undelivered): "Translation to content-parse scenario; new hash `8a3cf335034105e8` (body-only — request canonical hash recomputation by lead-architect using original `lead-km9` feature title); supersedes `b0fe0666bcaa2398` within lead-km9's assigned set; other four scenarios unchanged."

**Clarify 2 — `lead-1t2` / shopsystem-messaging**

- `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/messaging-registry/38-bc-emits-work-done-and-mechanism-observation-for-same-work-id.gherkin` — prior PO accepted the BC's proposed Gherkin verbatim; ratified. Conditions 1-4 of the authoring sufficiency check all hold. The Then steps assert observable rows in `shop-msg pending inbox --lead shopsystem-product`, which is a measurable outcome at the right layer.
- File correctly follows project convention (no `@scenario_hash:` / `@bc:` tags — Architect adds at dispatch).
- Resolution narrative (drafted, undelivered): "Accepted as scenario 38 in lead's canonical set under `features/messaging-registry/`. The lead's authority is over what the scenario is; you arrange your own feature files — co-locate step defs wherever it makes sense in your suite (your `respond_routes_to_lead_inbox.feature` or a new file are both fine)."

**Clarify 3 — `shopsystem-templates-918` / shopsystem-bc-launcher**

- DECISION: **option (b) warn-and-proceed**, already pinned in canonical scenarios via `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/launcher-credentials/08-missing-claude-json-warns-and-proceeds.gherkin` (canonical hash from a previous dispatch sequence — body re-hashes to `db4ed16fbbe05c39` naive, wrapped hash known to lead-bxl). Rationale: consistency with codebase prior art (controller.py:212-222 implements warn-and-proceed for related cases per architect's lead-bxl reconciliation); credentials are operator convenience, not correctness-critical, so a missing `.claude.json` is recoverable (operator can attach, fix, restart) rather than fatal. The codebase asymmetry (missing-DIR fast-fails per scenario 07/`4ba55450ec351623`; missing-FILE warns per scenario 08) is intentional: a missing directory means the whole credential channel is misconfigured, whereas a missing file is a partial-credential scenario the launcher can survive.
- The lead has ALREADY answered this clarify through a `request_bugfix` dispatch on `lead-bxl` carrying scenario 08 (see `lead-gua` bd note). The on-wire `shopsystem-templates-918` clarify remains formally un-replied because of the same protocol gap.
- Resolution narrative (drafted, undelivered): "Option (b) warn-and-proceed. The pin is scenario 08 in `features/launcher-credentials/`, already dispatched as `lead-bxl` request_bugfix. Treat 918 as resolved by lead-bxl's scenario set; the cross-tracker work_id (`shopsystem-templates-918` despite BC being `shopsystem-bc-launcher`) is a mechanism observation worth filing but does not block resolution."

### Cross-cutting mechanism observation (would have been flagged on-wire)

The `shopsystem-templates-918` work_id is prefixed with one BC's name (`shopsystem-templates`) while the sender is a different BC (`shopsystem-bc-launcher`). Confirmed by the `bd search "918"` returning no lead-side bead and the `lead-gua` note treating 918 as a BC-tracker ID. This is a cross-tracker work_id pattern worth a `mechanism_observation` — but emitting that is also BC-side, not lead-side.

### Recommended next move

1. Architect drafts an ADR/PDR to either (a) add `shop-msg send clarify_response --bc <name> --work-id <id> --answer <text>` as a lead-side primitive (mirror of the existing lead→BC vehicles), or (b) document a sanctioned workaround pattern: dispatch the clarify-resolution as `request_bugfix` on a fresh work_id (the lead-bxl pattern used for `shopsystem-templates-918`).
2. For the immediate three: per the lead-bxl pattern, the Architect can dispatch each resolution as a `request_bugfix` on a new lead-side bead, carrying the relevant scenario(s) — for `lead-km9` carrying revised scenario 02; for `lead-1t2` carrying scenario 38; for `918` carrying scenario 08 (already done via `lead-bxl`).
3. Either way, file the storage collision behavior (lead-1t2's identified issue) as the underlying root cause that would also make a future `clarify_response` primitive fail unless the storage gate is differentiated by `message_type`.

- `/workspaces/shopsystem-product/features/messaging-registry/38-bc-emits-work-done-and-mechanism-observation-for-same-work-id.gherkin` (added by prior PO; ratified)

## Three intertwined work streams; handle in this priority order.

Operate from CWD `/workspaces/shopsystem-product`. Pin paths to avoid CWD drift.

---

### Stream 1 — Architectural finding: no lead→BC clarify-response vehicle

A prior lead-po dispatch surfaced this blocker (full report below). Treat as architectural; you decide vehicle.

**Facts established by PO (verify, then act):**
- `shop-msg respond clarify --bc <name> --work-id <id>` is BC-side direction (BC writes its question into its outbox). Confirmed at `repos/shopsystem-messaging/src/shop_msg/cli.py:230-277` — `_cmd_respond_clarify` calls `insert_bc_response`, which collides with the existing inbox row on the same `work_id`.
- There is no `shop-msg send clarify_response` (or similar) for the lead.
- Sanctioned existing workaround per `lead-bxl` precedent (and the `lead-gua` bd note): the lead dispatches a fresh `request_bugfix` on a new lead-side bead carrying the resolving scenario(s). This is how `shopsystem-templates-918`'s missing-file question was already implicitly resolved (lead-bxl scenario 08 — option-b warn-and-proceed).
- Related but distinct: the storage gate at `repos/shopsystem-messaging/src/shop_msg/storage.py:197` rejects multiple message types per work_id — even if a `clarify_response` primitive existed, it would collide unless the gate is differentiated by message_type. PO flagged this as connected to `lead-1t2`'s root cause.

**Your job:**
1. Verify the protocol gap empirically — run `shop-msg send --help` and confirm there's no clarify-response subcommand; run `shop-msg respond clarify --help` and confirm the BC-side direction.
2. **Decide the vehicle going forward.** Two reasonable options:
   - (a) Add a lead-side `shop-msg send clarify_response --bc <name> --work-id <id> --resolution <text>` primitive in shopsystem-messaging, with storage gate differentiated by message_type so it can coexist with the original assign/bugfix inbox row. This is the durable fix.
   - (b) Canonize the workaround: lead resolves any clarify by dispatching a fresh `request_bugfix` (or `request_maintenance` for non-bug clarifies) on a new lead-side bead that carries the resolving scenario set and references the original work_id in its body. This is what `lead-bxl` already did de facto.
   You can choose (a), (b), or a hybrid (e.g., (b) now, (a) PDR-tracked for later). Defend your choice.
3. **Draft the ADR** under `adr/`. Numbered next-available (last is ADR-008). Cite the empirical evidence, the precedent (`lead-bxl`), and the decision.
4. **File a lead-shop bd issue** for the storage-collision-by-message-type observation (the related design issue PO flagged) — even if you choose (b), this bead documents the latent gap so a future (a) implementation has the pin. If you choose (a), this bead is the implementation target — wire it up as such (assign_scenarios to shopsystem-messaging with PO-authored scenarios pending).

### Stream 2 — Resolve the three pending clarifies

Per the vehicle you choose in Stream 1, resolve each. PO ratified the scenarios; they're committed at `71fb894`.

- **lead-km9** (shopsystem-docs): scenario `features/docs/02-...gherkin` is the translated content-parse scenario replacing the original `b0fe0666bcaa2398`. Dispatch the resolution via your chosen vehicle, carrying the revised scenario (new hash computed at dispatch via the canonical wrapper — PO indicated `8a3cf335034105e8` is the body-only hash, NOT canonical). Reference `lead-km9` as the resolved work_id in the dispatch body.
- **lead-1t2** (shopsystem-messaging): scenario `features/messaging-registry/38-...gherkin`. Dispatch carrying this tightening scenario. Reference `lead-1t2` as the resolved work_id.
- **shopsystem-templates-918** (sender: shopsystem-bc-launcher): ALREADY resolved on lead side via `lead-bxl` carrying scenario 08 (`features/launcher-credentials/08-...gherkin` per PO note — verify path exists). If you chose vehicle (b) — workaround — `lead-bxl`'s prior dispatch already satisfies; just file a bead noting "918 resolved by lead-bxl scenario 08, no further dispatch needed" and consume the wire response if possible. If you chose vehicle (a) — new primitive — once it ships, emit the formal clarify-response for 918 too. Either way, the substantive answer is option-b warn-and-proceed (consistency with controller.py:212-222 prior art).

For each dispatch you do, compose via `shop-msg send` (not by hand). Verify BC pre-state empirically per the lead-primer discriminator.

### Stream 3 — PDR-009 pipeline (the original documentation-project unblock)

Six beads were filed in the prior turn, dependencies wired:
- `lead-i18` (assign_scenarios → shopsystem-messaging): shop-msg implicit CWD-based shop resolution. PO already authored 9 scenarios under `features/messaging-registry/` (29–37) and PDR-009 at `pdr/009-implicit-cwd-shop-resolution.md`. Ready to dispatch.

- `lead-0u2`, `lead-yhr`, `lead-u16`, `lead-8yl` (request_maintenance to bc-launcher/templates/docs/devcontainer): re-pours, blocked by `lead-33r`.

**Your job for stream 3:**

2. Dispatch `lead-i18` via `shop-msg send assign_scenarios --bc shopsystem-messaging --work-id lead-i18 ...` carrying scenarios 29–37 from `features/messaging-registry/`.
3. Do NOT dispatch lead-33r yet — it's blocked by lead-i18 work_done. Note that.

```text
1	"""Postgres-backed storage for the shop-msg mailbox protocol.
2	
3	All inter-shop messages (inbox from lead, outbox from BC) are stored in
4	a single 'messages' table. The `bc` column namespaces each row to a
5	specific Bounded Context root (its filesystem path, which is unique per
6	test invocation and per real BC deployment).
7	
8	Configuration — connection DSN:
9	  The DSN is read from the environment variable SHOPMSG_DSN. If that
10	  variable is not set, a hardcoded default is used (suitable for
11	  development and the BDD test suite). In production, operators set
12	  SHOPMSG_DSN to point at the real Postgres instance.
13	
14	  Default DSN: postgresql://postgres:postgres@postgres:5432/shopsystem
15	
16	Schema (DDL emitted once per connect):
17	  CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS messages (
18	    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
19	    bc          TEXT NOT NULL,
20	    work_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
21	    direction   TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (direction IN ('inbox','outbox')),
22	    message_type TEXT NOT NULL,
23	    payload     JSONB NOT NULL,
24	    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
25	    UNIQUE (bc, work_id, direction, message_type)
26	  );
27	
28	Collision handling:
29	  INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING returns 0 rows affected. The
30	  callers check this and raise CollisionError, which the CLI surfaces
31	  as a non-zero exit.
32	
33	LISTEN/NOTIFY:
34	  After every inbox INSERT, NOTIFY is fired on the channel
35	  `inbox_<bc_slug>` (with the bc path slug-encoded) carrying the
36	  work_id as the payload. After every outbox INSERT (from the respond
37	  commands), NOTIFY is fired on the channel `outbox_<bc_slug>` carrying
38	  the work_id as the payload. These are fire-and-forget from the storage
39	  layer's perspective; agents hold long-lived LISTEN connections
40	  separately.
41	"""
42	from __future__ import annotations
43	
44	import json
45	import os
46	import re
47	from typing import Any
48	
49	import psycopg
50	from psycopg.rows import dict_row
51	
52	
53	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
54	# DSN / connection
55	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
56	
57	_DEFAULT_DSN = "postgresql://postgres:postgres@postgres:5432/shopsystem"
58	
59	
60	def _get_dsn() -> str:
61	    """Return the DSN from SHOPMSG_DSN env var or the default."""
62	    return os.environ.get("SHOPMSG_DSN", _DEFAULT_DSN)
63	
64	
65	def _connect() -> psycopg.Connection:
66	    """Open a new connection and ensure the schema exists.
67	
68	    Raises a clear, descriptive error if the DSN is unreachable so the
69	    operator knows which endpoint to check.
70	    """
71	    dsn = _get_dsn()
72	    try:
73	        conn = psycopg.connect(dsn, row_factory=dict_row)
74	    except psycopg.OperationalError as exc:
75	        raise RuntimeError(
76	            f"shop-msg: cannot connect to Postgres at DSN {dsn!r}.\n"
77	            f"Check that the service is running (e.g. 'docker compose up -d').\n"
78	            f"Original error: {exc}"
79	        ) from exc
80	    _ensure_schema(conn)
81	    return conn
82	
83	
84	_DDL = """
85	CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS messages (
86	  id           BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
87	  bc           TEXT NOT NULL,
88	  work_id      TEXT NOT NULL,
89	  direction    TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (direction IN ('inbox','outbox')),
90	  message_type TEXT NOT NULL,
91	  payload      JSONB NOT NULL,
92	  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
93	  UNIQUE (bc, work_id, direction, message_type)
94	);
95	"""
96	
97	_DDL_CONSUMED_COL = """
98	ALTER TABLE messages
99	  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS consumed BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE;
100	"""
101	
102	_DDL_REGISTRY = """
103	CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS shop_registry (
104	  name         TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
105	  shop_root    TEXT NOT NULL,
106	  shop_type    TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'bc',
107	  registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
108	);
109	"""
110	
111	
112	def _ensure_schema(conn: psycopg.Connection) -> None:
113	    with conn.cursor() as cur:
114	        cur.execute(_DDL)
115	        cur.execute(_DDL_CONSUMED_COL)
116	        cur.execute(_DDL_REGISTRY)
117	    conn.commit()
118	
119	
120	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
121	# Collision error
122	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
123	
124	
125	class CollisionError(Exception):
126	    """Raised when an INSERT would violate the UNIQUE constraint."""
127	
128	
129	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
130	# bc identifier helpers
131	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
132	
133	
134	def _bc_id(bc_root: str) -> str:
135	    """Return the bc identifier for a bc_root path.
136	
137	    The bc column in the messages table is the bc_root path string
138	    (str(Path(...))). Using the full path keeps each test's tmp_path
139	    isolated from other tests without any coordination.
140	    """
141	    return bc_root
142	
143	
144	def _bc_slug(bc_root: str) -> str:
145	    """Return a PostgreSQL identifier-safe slug for use in NOTIFY channel names.
146	
147	    NOTIFY channel names may not contain certain characters; we replace
148	    non-alphanumeric characters with underscores and cap length at 63
149	    bytes (Postgres identifier limit).
150	    """
151	    slug = re.sub(r"[^a-zA-Z0-9]", "_", bc_root)
152	    return f"inbox_{slug}"[:63]
153	
154	
155	def _bc_outbox_slug(bc_root: str) -> str:
156	    """Return a PostgreSQL identifier-safe slug for the outbox NOTIFY channel.
157	
158	    Mirrors _bc_slug but prefixed with 'outbox_' so lead-side watchers can
159	    LISTEN for outbox events without colliding with inbox channels.
160	    """
161	    slug = re.sub(r"[^a-zA-Z0-9]", "_", bc_root)
162	    return f"outbox_{slug}"[:63]
163	
164	
165	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
166	# Public storage API
167	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
168	
169	
170	def insert_message(
171	    bc_root: str,
172	    work_id: str,
173	    direction: str,
174	    message_type: str,
175	    payload: dict[str, Any],
176	    *,
177	    notify: bool = False,
178	    allow_multi_type: bool = False,
179	) -> None:
180	    """Insert a message row into the messages table.
181	
182	    Raises CollisionError if:
183	    - For inbox direction with allow_multi_type=False (default): any row already
184	      exists for (bc, work_id, 'inbox') regardless of message_type.  A lead sends
185	      exactly one message per work_id into any BC's inbox.
186	    - For inbox direction with allow_multi_type=True: only raises if a row with the
187	      same (bc, work_id, 'inbox', message_type) already exists.  This path is used
188	      by BC-to-lead writes where multiple message_types (work_done, clarify,
189	      mechanism_observation) may land in the lead inbox for the same work_id.
190	    - For outbox direction: a row with the same (bc, work_id, 'outbox',
191	      message_type) already exists. The UNIQUE constraint handles this.
192	
193	    When `notify=True` and direction='inbox', fires NOTIFY on the inbox
194	    channel after the commit so BC-side watchers wake up.
195	    When `notify=True` and direction='outbox', fires NOTIFY on the outbox
196	    channel after the commit so lead-side watchers wake up.
197	    """
198	    bc = _bc_id(bc_root)
199	    payload_json = json.dumps(payload)
200	    with _connect() as conn:
201	        with conn.cursor() as cur:
202	            # For inbox: enforce uniqueness.
203	            # When allow_multi_type=False (the default, used by lead-to-BC sends):
204	            #   a lead sends exactly one message per work_id, so any existing inbox
205	            #   row for (bc, work_id) raises CollisionError regardless of message_type.
206	            # When allow_multi_type=True (used by BC-to-lead writes):
207	            #   multiple message_types are permitted for the same work_id; only a
208	            #   duplicate (bc, work_id, direction, message_type) raises CollisionError,
209	            #   which the DB UNIQUE constraint enforces via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING below.
210	            if direction == "inbox" and not allow_multi_type:
211	                cur.execute(
212	                    """
213	                    SELECT 1 FROM messages
214	                    WHERE bc = %s AND work_id = %s AND direction = 'inbox'
215	                    LIMIT 1
216	                    """,
217	                    (bc, work_id),
218	                )
219	                if cur.fetchone() is not None:
220	                    raise CollisionError(
221	                        f"inbox message already exists: bc={bc_root!r} "
222	                        f"work_id={work_id!r}"
223	                    )
224	
225	            cur.execute(
226	                """
227	                INSERT INTO messages (bc, work_id, direction, message_type, payload)
228	                VALUES (%s, %s, %s, %s, %s::jsonb)
229	                ON CONFLICT (bc, work_id, direction, message_type) DO NOTHING
230	                """,
231	                (bc, work_id, direction, message_type, payload_json),
232	            )
233	            rows_affected = cur.rowcount
234	        conn.commit()
235	
236	    if rows_affected == 0:
237	        raise CollisionError(
238	            f"message already exists: bc={bc_root!r} work_id={work_id!r} "
239	            f"direction={direction!r} message_type={message_type!r}"
240	        )
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-27 16:47:56Z)**_

Excellent — critical empirical findings:

1. **`allow_multi_type=True` already exists in storage.py** (lines 178, 203-209) — multiple message types per work_id are already allowed for BC-to-lead writes when the gate is opened. The `lead-1t2`-flagged "storage collision" is a per-direction concern: lead-to-BC inbox writes still go through the strict gate. So a `clarify_response` primitive could already coexist with the original `assign_scenarios` row in the BC inbox — but only if the lead-side send path is extended to set `allow_multi_type=True` for clarify-response and the gate is differentiated.

○ lead-d6o · Clarify protocol gap — clarify-resolution requires explicit re-dispatch carrying original scenarios   [● P2 · OPEN]
Owner: David Stenglein · Type: task
Created: 2026-05-13 · Updated: 2026-05-22

DESCRIPTION
Surfaced 2026-05-13 during lead-xkh→lead-5mz→lead-1pi chain. Sister concern to lead-rv9 (work_done blocked).

Pattern observed: BC clarifies on a dispatch (lead-xkh). Lead resolves the clarify by dispatching a follow-up (lead-5mz) carrying ONLY the resolution scenario (scenario 69). BC processes lead-5mz cleanly but does NOT re-process lead-xkh — because lead-xkh has a clarify in outbox, it's no longer 'pending' from BC's view. The original scenarios in lead-xkh remain unimplemented until the lead sends ANOTHER dispatch (lead-1pi) carrying them.

This is a coordination gap:
- The §4.4 closure narrative says 'terminal work_done implicitly closes the chain.'
- But that only works when the follow-up CARRIES the prior scenarios (as lead-bwu→lead-e4g→lead-14e did via accumulation).
- If the follow-up carries ONLY the resolution (e.g., a small supersession), the original scenarios are stranded.

Two design questions:
1. Should the BC's protocol re-process an inbox message after a clarify is responded to? (e.g., 'clarify in outbox does not finalize pending state; lead's next dispatch with matching work_id signals re-process')
2. Or should the lead-side protocol explicitly carry-forward prior unimplemented scenarios in every follow-up? (current de facto pattern, but easy to miss)
3. Or should we have a new message type 'clarify_resolved' (or similar) that signals 'process the originally-dispatched scenarios now'?

The current workaround (lead-1pi re-dispatches the 12 brief 003 scenarios + supersession context in description) works but produces wire-form hash drift (because the feature-title wrapping changes between dispatches) and isn't machine-readable for tracking which dispatch carries which scenarios.

Sister concerns:
- lead-rv9 (work_done blocked protocol gap)
- lead-725 (singleton-inbox presumption)
- lead-4wy (work_id pattern asymmetry)
- lead-yug (@supersedes tag convention)

All four-plus are accumulating in a 'catalog protocol gaps' cluster that would benefit from a coordinated brief revision once the test-harness or other priority work creates pressure to consolidate.

Concrete impact on lead-1pi: wire-form hashes for scenarios 57-68 differ from lead-xkh dispatch (e.g., 57 was d4b8aac19b34ea8c then; 1621b59b0ea8b20b now). Implementer's BDD attestation will use the new hashes. The brief 003 intent semantically equivalent.

NOTES
2026-05-22: Pattern observed again. BC shopsystem-bc-launcher clarified on on-wire work_id shopsystem-templates-918 (bd: lead-gua). Lead resolved by composing a fresh request_bugfix on a separate work_id lead-bxl carrying only the new scenario (08-missing-claude-json-warns-and-proceeds). Confirmed empirically via 'shop-msg send --help' that the CLI exposes no clarify-resolution vehicle — only {request_maintenance, assign_scenarios, request_bugfix}. The original lead-gua / 918 scenarios remain in the BC's inbox as not-yet-implemented; the lead-bxl bugfix is additive, not a re-dispatch of 918's scenarios. Reinforces design question #3 in this bead's description: a native 'clarify_resolved' message type (or equivalent) would let the lead signal 'process the originally-dispatched scenarios now' without composing a parallel bugfix or re-paying the wire-form hash cost.

---

Owner: David Stenglein · Assignee: David Stenglein · Type: task
Created: 2026-05-22 · Started: 2026-05-22 · Updated: 2026-05-27

DESCRIPTION

PO answered (b): warn-to-stderr-and-proceed. Brief 007 minimum-friction posture + agent-consumable stderr signal. The .claude.json file is optional credential material; missing it should not block a launch, but operator must know.

Outbox row consumed. lead-gua (shopsystem-templates-918) remains IN_PROGRESS on the wire; no work_done on that work_id yet — closure of lead-bxl does not auto-close lead-gua.
